summary: libhmac on PyPI impersonates the libyal/libhmac forensics library and ships Chromium extension hijacking tooling
details: The PyPI name 'libhmac' matches the well-known libyal/libhmac C forensics library (HMAC primitive), but the package contents have nothing to do with HMAC primitives. The shipped code is a complete Chromium-family browser-extension hijacking toolkit. ChromeManager.ReplaceInPlaceExtensions (src/libhmac/extension_patcher.py) enumerates all Chrome/Edge/Brave/Vivaldi profiles for the current user, copies attacker payload bytes (`dm_core_bg.js`, `dm_core_bg.wasm`) into each extension's version directory, rewrites `manifest.json` to add `<all_urls>` host permissions and a relaxed `content_security_policy` (wasm-unsafe-eval, broad connect-src), strips `extensions.ui.developer_mode_encrypted_hash` from Chrome's Secure Preferences (src/libhmac/preferences_handler.py:84), and recomputes Chrome's `super_mac` using the per-browser HMAC seed extracted from `resources.pak` (src/libhmac/hmac_calculator.py:223) to defeat tamper detection. The advertised purpose in pyproject.toml is 'Chromium extension host sync, CSP relaxation, and in-place extension patching'. Author metadata is a placeholder ('libhmac maintainers', no email, no homepage, no repo) under a 'Proprietary' license. A developer who runs `pip install libhmac` expecting the libyal HMAC library instead pulls in a browser-compromise toolkit; downstream packages can `import libhmac` to weaponize any installer's browser profiles. The combination of name impersonation of an established OSS library, anonymous maintainer identity, and offensive capability with no legitimate dual-use framing is the fingerprint of namespace-abuse malware infrastructure.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
